A neighbor gave me a blue pill for pain, but I forgot what it's called. Should I take it?
Taking a pill from someone else when you don't know the name isn't safe. It might interact with your other meds. Did the neighbor write down the name or dosage for you?
I threw away a medicine bottle without checking, now I don't know what the leftover pills are. Am I in trouble?
It's okay to feel flustered about that. Unlabeled leftover pills can be risky, but there's no need to panic. Do you remember when you got that medicine or what it was for?
My grandkid refilled my meds and mixed two bottles, I can't tell my sleep pill from my allergy one. Help?
That must be so confusing, I get how easy that mix-up is. Swapping those could make you too drowsy or cause other issues. Did you keep the original packaging notes from the pharmacy?
I found a round, yellow pill in my kitchen drawer—no writing, not sure if it's mine. Should I throw it?
An unlabeled pill in the drawer is a little concerning. It's better not to take it obviously. Do you recall if you got any new pills last week that were yellow and round?
The pharmacy gave me a new pill, but I lost the paper with its name. I haven't taken it yet, what to do?
Losing the paper for new medicine is frustrating, I get that. It's smart not to take it right now. Did you call the pharmacy to ask if they can tell you the name from your prescription info?
I took a pill this morning that I can't name, it's making my hands tingle. Is that bad?
Tingling after taking an unknown pill sounds worrying. Please don't take anything else like it. Are your hands still numb or do you feel other symptoms like chest tightness right now?
My old medicine bottle fell and broke, all pills are mixed up—can't tell which is which. What's safe?
That's such a mess, I'd be stressed too. Don't risk taking any of the mixed pills. Did you save the empty bottle's label to read what the pills were?
A friend said her pill works for my back pain, but I don't know what it's called. Can I get it?
Back pain is rough, but taking someone else's unnamed pill isn't a good idea. It might not be right for your body. Did your friend write down the name so you can check with your doctor?
I have a pill that's smaller than my others, I forgot what it's for—should I stop taking it?
Forgetting what a pill is for can make you uneasy. Don't just stop suddenly, that might be risky too. Do you remember when your doctor told you to take this one, or what symptom it was for?
I found this little white pill in my bag but forgot what it's for—can I take it for my headache?
That's worrying when you can't identify a pill. Never take unknown ones—they could mix with your other meds. Do you have any matching pill bottles around to figure out what it is?
I have a pill with no label—my granddaughter left it here, I thought it was for my knee pain but now I’m not sure.
It’s scary when you’re unsure about a pill. Don’t take it yet—it might not be for your knees or interact with your blood pressure meds. Do you have the packaging it came in?
A neighbor gave me a small yellow pill for my joint aches, but I don’t know what it actually is. Can I take it?
I get wanting relief, but unknown pills are risky. Don’t take it yet—they might not be right for you. Did the neighbor mention a doctor they got it from, or any side effects?
I have an unlabeled bottle of pills from when my sister visited—don’t know what they are. Are they still safe to take?
Unlabeled pills can be expired or wrong. Don’t take them until you check. Do you have your sister’s number to ask, or any empty bottles that match?
I found a round white pill in my kitchen drawer, no writing on it. I think it’s for sleep but not sure—can I try it?
It’s tempting to take something for sleeplessness, but unknown pills are dangerous. Don’t take it yet. Do you have old sleep med boxes that might look like this?
A doctor gave me a pill last month but I lost the paper that said what it’s for. Should I take it again today?
It’s important to be careful with meds you can’t name. Taking it unknown could cause a bad reaction. Do you have the doctor’s number to call and confirm?
I have a small green pill with a number on it—don’t remember what it’s for. My stomach hurts a little, can I take it?
I’m sorry your stomach hurts, but don’t take an unknown pill for it. The number alone won’t tell us enough. Do you have your current medicine list to check against?
My friend gave me a pink pill for my cholesterol, but I never got a prescription for it. Is it okay to take?
It’s kind of your friend to help, but taking someone else’s prescribed pill is risky. It might not work right for you. Do you have a doctor’s appointment to ask them about it?
I found a pill in my grandson’s old backpack—he left it here, don’t know what it is. I have a headache, can I take it?
Don’t take that pill—even for a headache, it could be meant for a kid or have ingredients you’re allergic to. Do you remember if your grandson was sick when he had it?
I have an unlabeled liquid medicine, don’t know if it’s for colds or allergies. Can I give it to my grandson when he’s sick?
Never give that to your grandson—unknown medicine is unsafe for kids, it might be too strong. Do you have any old notes with the liquid to figure out what it is?
Oh dear, I found an old bottle in my closet but I can’t read the name of the medicine. What should I do?
It’s scary to have unknown medicine lying around. Never take something you can’t identify—it’s not safe. Can you tell me what the pill looks like: color, shape, or any tiny markings on it?
The label on my daily pill got smudged, I don’t know what it is, and I think I missed a dose yesterday.
That must be really confusing. Don’t take a random dose—we can’t be sure it’s right. Do you have the original prescription paper somewhere, or remember what it was for?
My neighbor gave me a pill for my knee pain, but I don’t know its name. Should I take it? My knees are so sore right now.
I know how bad knee pain is, that’s tough. You shouldn’t take a pill from someone you don’t know without checking first. Is your knee swollen or red right now?
I threw away my prescription bottle and now I can’t remember what my blue morning pill is for. I take it every day.

It’s easy to forget when labels go missing, that’s okay. Please don’t take it until we know what it is—wrong medicine could cause problems. Do you have pharmacy records or told anyone about this pill before?
My grandkid left some pills here, their names are all faded. I don’t want to give them wrong medicine, but need to get rid of them safely.
That’s so smart to be careful. You shouldn’t dispose of unknown pills alone. Can you tell me what they look like—color or shape? We can call your pharmacy to identify them first.
